segments.text: Welcome to Lecture 2. Today we will discuss consistency models and their importance in database systems.

ACID is a set of properties that ensure database transactions are processed reliably. Atomicity ensures that all or nothing is committed, consistency ensures data remains consistent, independence ensures concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other, and durability ensures once committed, the transaction is stable.

There are two types of consistency models: strong consistency and weak consistency. Strong consistency ensures all nodes have the same data at the same time, while weak consistency allows for a delay between updates on different nodes.

When multiple clients write to the same data simultaneously, conflict resolution techniques are used. Locks and compare-and-set operations assume a single up-to-date copy of the data, while version vectors track changes made by each client.

Isolation levels control how concurrent transactions interact with each other. From weakest to strongest, the isolation levels are: read uncommitted, read committed, repeatable read, and serializable.
A monotonic clock measures time in a consistent manner. NTP synchronizes clocks across systems to ensure accurate timekeeping.
In distributed systems, quorum consistency ensures data is available on a majority of nodes. Key range partitioning divides data into ranges to reduce contention.
Data replication involves copying data across multiple nodes. Master-slave replication uses a single leader node, while multi-leader replication allows concurrent writes.
Partitioning schemes divide data into ranges or groups. Hash partitioning uses a hash function to determine the range, while range partitioning divides data based on a key.
